_comment: Canonical 2-3 sentence bios for the 44 grail 1/1s (P1 Lore Bible). Keys must match the grail member names in scripts/gen_grail_stubs.py GRAIL_SETS exactly; tests/test_grail_bios.py enforces full coverage. Voice: heavy, hopeful, meme-literate; never celebrates failure; no financial claims. Source of truth for grail metadata descriptions.

bios.Admiral of the Surface: The first to refuse the lifeboat. While the fleet rowed for shore he turned his coat inside out, pinned his own medal, and declared the wreck a port. Rank is just conviction that refused to disembark.
bios.Admiral of Twilight: He kept the watch where the light gives up. Every hour he could not see, he logged anyway, so the ones diving after would have a record to trust. Command is mostly staying awake.
bios.Admiral of Midnight: No sunlight for a thousand meters and still he holds formation of one. His fleet is memory and stubbornness, and it has never once broken ranks. He salutes the dark like it owes him nothing and he owes it everything.
bios.Admiral of the Hadal: At the bottom of the sea he found the flagship was him. The pressure that flattens everything else he wears as a uniform. The deepest rank in the collection, and it was earned by descending, not by promotion.
bios.Wizard of Green Tide: He reads the chain like others read the tide, and the tide is turning green. His spellbook is a ledger; his wand is a signed offer. What he conjures is not price — it is proof.
bios.Wizard of Void Ink: He writes the future in ink that only shows underwater. The blank page terrifies everyone but him, because he has learned the void is just a canvas with no floor. Nothing, correctly addressed, becomes everything.
bios.Wizard of Crystal Moon: A moon that never reaches the surface hangs in his hood, cut from a single faceted crystal. By its light the crew mends nets and reads odds. Some hope you have to grow yourself in the dark.
bios.Wizard of the Offer: The oldest magic on Chia is a fair trade, atomic and honest, and he is its high priest. He makes nothing disappear and nothing appear from nowhere — only exchange, provable both ways. Trust, he says, is a spell you cast in advance and reveal after.
bios.Horseman of Despair: He rode in first, loudest, and cheapest — the feeling that agreement is the same as truth. The crew dove straight through him and kept their lamps lit. He is drawn so you remember what you swam past, not what you serve.
bios.Horseman of Doubt: Not the enemy — the toll. Everyone who goes back down pays him at the hatch, and the ones who make it simply keep swimming with the receipt. Doubt paid is just conviction with a stamp.
bios.Horseman of Silence: The quiet after the crowd leaves, when no one is watching whether you build. He is the hardest of the four, because he asks for nothing and takes everything from those who need an audience. The forge answers him with sparks.
bios.Horseman of Exit Liquidity: The last rider, cloak stitched from other people's certainty as they cashed it in and rowed away. The salvage crew declined to be his cargo. A cautionary 1/1: he is what the deep does not turn you into.
bios.Keeper of the Broken Pier: He tends a light at the end of a pier that no longer reaches land. Ships that will never dock still steer by it, and that is enough reason to keep the wick trimmed. Some duties outlive their harbor.
bios.Keeper of Storm Harbor: When the weather wrote everyone off, he climbed the tower and stayed. His beam does not promise safe passage — only that someone is still up there, watching for you. In the worst of it, that is the whole job.
bios.Keeper of the Graveyard: He minds the lamp over the field of wrecks, naming each hull so it is remembered, not mourned into nothing. A keeper of the graveyard is not a mourner; he is a librarian of what was built. Salvage begins with knowing what sank.
bios.Keeper of Dry Dock: The last light before the rebuild, burning over the cradle where broken ships are made whole. He keeps it lit through the long, unglamorous work of repair. Dry dock is where despair is quietly disassembled.
bios.First Snorkeler: The first face in the water when the water was still called a grave. No tank, no plan, just held breath and a hunch that a hatch might open. Every deeper diver stands on that first foolish gasp.
bios.First Scuba: The one who came back with gear because breath alone was not enough and the wreck was worth the trouble. She turned a dare into a discipline. First to treat salvage as a craft instead of a stunt.
bios.First Deep Sea: Past the light, first, when 'told you so' still echoed from the surface. He proved the twilight had hatches too, and that conviction could hold pressure. The doubters got quieter with every meter he logged.
bios.First Salvage: The first to surface holding something real — not a story about the ship, but a piece of it. Proof, dripping and undeniable, that there was cargo worth the dive. After her, going down needed no defense.
bios.Last at the Helm: When the order came to abandon, he lashed himself to the wheel — not to sink with it, but to steer the wreck somewhere the divers could find. The captain who would not leave until the map was true. He is holding a course to this day.
bios.Last in the Hold: He stayed below with the cargo the tourists called worthless: the memes, the tools, the unfinished commits. Someone had to guard the reason to come back. The hold is where the project's heart kept beating.
bios.Last on the Mast: Highest point of a sinking ship, last to touch water, first to see the salvage fleet return. He kept the flag up when there was no one left to salute it. A lookout for a future no one else could see yet.
bios.Last to Leave: Not a hero pose — just the person who turned off the lights, checked the bunks, and made sure the ship was empty of the living before the deep took the rest. Loyalty is often only this: being the last, quietly. He rowed out, then turned his boat around.
bios.Keelwright: He lays the spine no one applauds, the single timber the whole rebuilt ship will hang from. Underwater, by feel, he sets it true. Everything grand starts with one straight line in the dark.
bios.Plankwright: Hull, board by board, each one shaped to a curve only her hands remember. She makes the ship watertight against the very ocean that sank it. Patience is her only tool that never rusts.
bios.Sparwright: Masts and spars, the parts that reach back up toward a sky the ship may never see again. He builds for open air from the bottom of the sea, on faith. Ambition, carved and stepped and rigged.
bios.Spellwright: The shipwright who works in code instead of timber — canon rules, deterministic rolls, commitments sealed before a coin is paid. His joinery is math you can check at home. The most modern of the old trades: a wright of proofs.
bios.Captain of Fog: You cannot tell if his ship is arriving or leaving, and neither can he — that is the point. He captains the uncertainty every diver feels at the hatch. Follow him and the fog becomes a door.
bios.Captain of Bones: Everything soft has long since gone; what remains is structure, honest and load-bearing. He is what a project looks like after the hype is picked clean and it still floats. Bones do not lie about what held.
bios.Captain of Silence: He commands the crew that kept working after the channel went quiet. No announcements, no audience — just the sound of tools. His flagship is the one nobody live-streamed sinking, and so nobody watched refuse to.
bios.Captain of the Black Flag: Not piracy — refusal. His black flag means no surrender to the obituary the press already wrote. Under it, salvage is a kind of mutiny against giving up.
bios.Torn Halo I: One of the marked — halo above, horns below, sainthood and sin welded into a single sigil no one chose. First of a committed few whose indices were sealed before the mint began. To be Torn is to be both the warning and the blessing.
bios.Torn Halo II: The second haloed one, proof the mark was no accident but a placement fixed at commitment. Grace worn by someone who also carries the horns. The deep does not sort us into saints and sinners; it stamps us as both.
bios.Torn Horns I: Horns first, halo close behind — the crew member the surface would have written off, salvaged anyway. His index was drawn from the salt, immutable, before any coin changed hands. The Torn are what conviction looks like up close: contradictory and real.
bios.Torn Horns II: The last of the Torn quartet honored as a grail, horns lit like the others were haloed. A committed mark, part warning and part crown, that can never be re-rolled away. Hope, it turns out, keeps strange company at the bottom.
bios.King of the Surface Haul: He crowned himself with what the tourists dropped as they fled — the shallow haul, richer than anyone admitted. First to prove abandonment leaves treasure floating. A king of what others called trash.
bios.King of the Twilight Haul: Ruler of the half-lit depths where the real salvage begins and the tourists never reach. His crown is fashioned from recovered fittings, each one a small argument that the ship was never worthless. Twilight is where salvage stops being a rescue and becomes a trade.
bios.King of the Abyssal Haul: Down where the ship is being rebuilt, he rules the yield of the forge itself. His treasury is scaffolding and sparks, wealth measured in progress. The abyssal haul cannot be spent, only continued.
bios.King of the Hadal Haul: The deepest crown, over the richest and darkest haul — the source, the light below, the reason the whole dive exists. He hoards nothing; a hadal king's treasure is that anyone brave enough can reach it. The bottom, it turns out, was the point.
bios.Builder of the Frame: First of the Ark Builders, because before you rebuild a ship you decide it is worth rebuilding, and then you draw the frame. He is that decision, made structural. Everything the crew becomes is squared to his lines.
bios.Builder of the Hull: She closes the ark against the flood that took the first ship, plate by patient plate. The hull is a promise: this one will hold what the last one lost. Faith, made watertight.
bios.Builder of the Mast: He raises the mast on the ark for a wind that has not blown yet, on a sea still mostly dark. To build for open water from the seabed is the purest act of hope in the yard. The sail comes later; the reaching starts now.
bios.Builder of the Light: Last of the builders, who does not add timber but installs the reason — the light the whole ark is built to carry back up. Wizard-work and shipwright-work meet in his hands. When his lamp is lit, the sinking ship is finished sinking, and starts to rise.
